speaker: interviewer
text: --1995. We left off on the last tape talking about how you got yourself in the corner, and you were able to remove the barbed wire from the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The windows.

speaker: interviewer
text: --window. Now, how was it that the German guard didn't see you doing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, there were no guards inside the-- the cars. There was-- at the end of the-- of the whole train, there was one car where the-- the guards were. They were not on the-- on the train on-- on each of the cars that we were in. They wouldn't want to be there. Anyway, after we managed to remove that barbed wire from the windows, we looked around and we asked people that would want to jump the train. And we would help, because you cannot jump from a window which is that high from a moving train without help.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there were absolutely no one [INAUDIBLE] to go. So there were four people of us left, three others and myself, and we decided to jump. How do you jump from this train? You have to lift a person, put his feet out first, and then go out of the window and hold-- hold yourself at the-- the ledge of the-- of that window and push yourself away from the train and jump.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we made a signal between us that whoever would jump first would go forward, and so-- and the-- the-- the last one would go backward so that we would meet later on. In fact, we all four jumped successfully, and we didn't get seriously hurt, were minor, minor scratches on our hands and our face. And we met after maybe a half hour of our jump.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the first one to jump?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the first one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were friends-- were these friends of yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were just people--

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they--

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you have met on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: They're people-- yes. I-- I didn't know them before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the last person that jumped-- I'm just curious-- did somebody helped him out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, for helping is-- is there are plenty of people who--

speaker: interviewer
text: People to help.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to help. There was no-- not a question about that. But people didn't want to take the risk and maybe because people have-- had family there, and they didn't want to leave them. I-- I-- I probably wouldn't have acted that way if I had my family there. I wouldn't have left them and jumped. But since I was not attached on that train-- and I remembered the promise I made to my wife. I said this is-- this is the opportunity, and-- and I'll do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, after we met, now we try to figure out a strategy-- what do we do next? It was night. We were just wandering around in the woods. We were in the middle of nowhere in the woods. And then by dawn, we saw some-- in-- in the far distance we saw houses where some lights were flickering and going out in the windows. So we could identify them as-- as houses. People lived there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we decided to knock at one of those doors and-- and-- and ask them for help. First of all, we needed food, because we didn't really take along any-- any food, you know. It was, as I said, I had a little suitcase, which is maybe, you know, this-- two-- two-- two by four, and I jumped with that suitcase. I had it with me, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we ventured into one of the houses. And they-- they suspected, but we didn't tell them, of course, that we are Jews. We told them-- because this was also a time when the Germans were taking Pole-- Polish people, transporting them to-- to Germany for-- for-- for labor. They needed workers. So we told him that we are Polish people who jumped the train. Whether they believed or not, it didn't matter.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they treated us well. They gave us some warm milk and bread. We had some money. We paid. And-- and we moved on. After two days of wandering around in the-- in the woods, we stumbled on one peasant family who was ready to take us in and give us shelter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were four, so he put us up in the attic. He had some hay there. And he said he will provide us with as much food as he will be able to-- to get. And we-- we said, we are going to pay, because we-- we had money on us. But he-- after a short while, a day or two, he said he cannot provide for four people, so he split us up. He took two people to-- to one of his relatives down the road, and two were-- stayed here. But after six days, I said to myself, it's time to move. I have to go back to keep my promise to my wife. And having no papers at all, no identification, we-- I went through the woods to the railroad station because--

speaker: interviewer
text: By yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: By myself. I left the others there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of the men you were with?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But I-- I remember the-- the place. The name of the place was [? Rogozno. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The town, yeah, the-- the-- the village, a very, very tiny village.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the name of the peasant?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I don't think that I knew the name. I don't think that I knew the name, because we didn't-- it didn't come to-- to exchange these niceties and to introduce yourself. My name is this and this. He didn't know mine, and I didn't know his. It wasn't necessary for our relationship that we know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went by yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, yeah, went back, and I-- I found myself through the woods to the railroad station. And I boarded the train, and I came back to Debica. It was nightfall. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't have to describe to you my feelings, my-- my fears, on that train, because there were very often the Germans came and examined papers and all this. They were checking out on people riding these trains. But it went through without an incident, and I arrived at Debica on the evening. And I went to-- to the ghetto, but there was no ghetto. There was a small area, three houses, surrounded with barbed wire. Before that, there was no-- nothing. It-- it was, you know, there was no barbed wire before. But then, all of a sudden, then I knew that this is-- must be the ghetto that was reduced. They were talking about reducing the ghetto. And I found a hole in that-- in one of the-- in the-- in the barbed wire, and I went in to the first house. And I told them who I am-- oh-- and I'm looking for my wife. Oh, your wife is here in the-- the next house, and they took me there. And when she saw me, she fainted, because she didn't imagine that I would escape, and I-- I would be here. She-- she-- she gave me up for dead. And we were reunited for a very short time. And at the-- we used that-- I couldn't show myself in the ghetto, make myself, my presence, known because I was not legal there. I was not supposed to be there. While the others were out-- went out to work every day, I was hiding in-- in-- in that-- someplace in-- in the cellar. And then we found somebody who was able to furnish us with-- with false identity cards. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we bought these identity cards for myself and for my wife. And I knew that it's dangerous to stay in this ghetto. I knew this is not going to last, because there's no reason they would leave 250 people and isolated there, that eventually they will ship us. It's a-- it was a matter of time. After we got these identity cards, we left the ghetto. We went to the railroad station. We are going to go to Cracow. In Cracow there was the ghetto already, and I had two brothers there. So I thought, maybe I'll be able to get into the ghetto in Cracow. It's a large ghetto, and I have my brothers. I-- I feel a little more secure.

speaker: interviewer
text: While we were waiting for the train there was an inspection of identity cards, and you can imagine our heart beating. How-- what-- how they will accept our brand-new identity cards? But they passed inspection. Did you have a different name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, a different name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jan [? Migawa ?] was my name. And we passed inspection, and we safely arrived in Cracow-- actually not in Cracow. It was a suburb of Cracow, because the train didn't go all the way to Cracow. And at this time, there was a curfew. After 9 o'clock, you shouldn't be seen in the street. So we couldn't leave the train station and go to the ghetto because it was after 9 o'clock. So I remembered that we met a Polish family [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who lived in this-- in this-- in this little-- in this suburb of Cracow. And I didn't know his address. I knew his name. I asked one of the workers at the railroad whether he knows this name. He said, yes, I know him very well, and I can lead you there to him. And-- and he led us to him, and we knocked at the door. When he saw us, he was-- he wasn't glad to see us, because he knew that he-- he's going to be exposed to some kind-- to some dangers if he-- but he-- he said, I will let you in for tonight. And tomorrow, we'll see what-- we will talk, and we'll see what we can do for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he let us in, and I showed him that the papers that I-- that we have. He said, OK, I-- I have an idea. There's a factory making pails, pails out of-- or tin pails, and he was employed in that factory. He worked in the office. They are in need of working people, because they're all-- they're all-- you know, everybody is-- is involved with the-- with the war effort. And I'm sure if you go there, they will hire you. So I did. And they said, yes, we will hire you. We need workers. But you need to have a-- a designation by the employment office. We cannot just hire you just like this. You have to go through the-- the-- the employment agency. It was a-- a official employment office. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't think twice. I went to the employment office, and I got the designation to this factory. I said, here they need work, and I want to work. And-- and I got it right away. This was the only document that was not a false document, by the way, because it was a real document issued by the employment office.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your wife also [INAUDIBLE] work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she didn't. She-- she had-- has a different story. I'll come to it in a minute. When I presented the-- this paper, of course they hired me. And this company had a place, a-- a house, where they could accommodate people who worked in their factory but did-- did not live in the-- in the close vicinity. They could accommodate them, you know, a room to sleep or to share with somebody. And they said, we have a-- a house for-- for our workers. And if you don't have where to stay, you can stay there. And I was-- I couldn't be happier than this. My wife stayed behind with this-- with this young man, with his family, and she was looking for a job through the papers. There were advertisements. And she answered one ad that required a maid for-- a maid and a-- a governess for children. She reported it. It happened to be a German family, and they hired her. And they took her into their house, and she was taking care of the children and cleaning the house, whatever was necessary. So for a little while, we were-- we were secure. And since we wanted to see each other, Sunday, there was a-- a day off work. She had also a day off. We used to-- they had a telephone and-- and-- where my wife was working, this family, they had a telephone. They were Germans. And I could call her on the telephone, and we made up-- we used to meet on Sunday afternoon to see each other. And this was going on for at least three months. Everything seemed to be working very well. After the end of that period, one afternoon I come back from work. I go into my room. There's a knock at the door, and two uniformed Germans come in-- the SD. This is short for Sicherheitsdienst-- Security Police. And when they come in, they look at me-- papers. I showed him my papers. He tore it up, says, you are a Jew. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. You are-- you are Jewish. I started to deny it, and he said, let your pants down. They examined me. And-- but they didn't hit me. They treated me civilly, you know, civilized. They told me to go get my belongings and go with them. And I-- I-- I got my belongings. I had the little valise, and I-- I was walking with them. This was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about 1/2 a mile, 3/4 of a mile, from where the ghetto was in-- in Cracow-- in Podgorze, actually. The ghetto was not in Cracow. It was over on the other side of the-- of the river. It's called Podgorze. And they were walking with me, flanking me. I was walking with that little valise, and it was in the afternoon about-- around 5 o'clock. There were [? kommandos-- ?] we were passing Plaszow concentration camp. There were [? kommandos ?] going out from Plaszow concentration camp to work in the ghetto. Because this was exactly three weeks after the liquidation of the Cracow ghetto. The-- the Cracow was-- the ghetto was liquidated March 13, 1943, and they caught me the end of March. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were leading me to the-- yeah, what-- what was left after the liquidation of the ghetto was the jail. Because they were-- they were catching people every day on false papers, like myself, or people hiding in the bunkers. And they were putting, you know, they're keeping on this jail for-- for-- for-- to be able to accommodate these people who they're catching on the-- on false papers. And there were some Jewish policemen in charge of that. They called them Ordnungsdienst in German. They were Jewish. They were in uniforms. They're working for the Germans, and they were taking care of the jail. While they were leading me, when we were walking, there was this [? kommando ?] going back, after the day's work, going back to the Plaszow concentration camp. And my brother, who was in that-- in that company, in that, you know, in that [? kommando, ?] which is going to the-- to work in the-- in the ghetto, saw me-- saw me and that these-- these two Germans are leading me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So many strange coincidences. It's not that they could do anything about me. One of-- the-- the brother that lives now in Israel, he saw me actually walking with these two Germans. The next day, he came down again with the [? kommando ?] to work. So he tried to get in touch with me in the jail, and he said, listen, I will try, maybe we can get you out, you know. But nothing happened. So what did happen is, after day 6, the-- the jail was filled up to capacity. They took-- they said, we are going to-- to-- we are going all to Plaszow. You know, you will walk. We'll march to Plaszow. And they already have the escort. They took us out from the jail and formed us in-- in-- in, you know, in five in a row, and we were walking the streets towards Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came in inside the gates of-- of the Plaszow consolation camp, there were two very large holes dug, tremendous holes, like bigger than this house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we didn't know what this is all about. The Lagerkommandant-- this-- I can tell you the name. This was Goeth, Amon Goeth, the-- the one who appears in He came out and told everybody-- not his helpers, of course, the Jewish policemen-- everybody to form a regular, you know, fall in-- in-- into five in a row.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was walking from one to one asking, what profession do you have? He, Goeth himself, ask everybody. I said, I'm a mechanic. So here there was a segregation right there. Some people to the left, some people to the right, not-- not unlike in something Mengele used to do in-- in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was selected to the-- to-- to the-- I don't remember whether it was the left or the right-- to one side. It turned out that 67 people out of more than 300 in that whole group were selected to live. And the others had to undress, move towards the edge of these holes, and they were shot down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We see. We saw that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I was among the 67 that was-- that survived that massacre at this time. Then I was integrated, of course, at Plaszow, and-- and I was put to work. First, I was working in the-- in the laundry. They had a-- a large barrack where they did laundry for-- for the officers and for the-- the guards and everybody, and they put me to work there. Then they put me to-- they took me out of this-- of this work detail, and they put me into a barracks where they had a-- they made handles to axes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Handles to what?

speaker: interviewee
text: To axes, to ax. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Why don't-- don't I-- the, you know, wooden handles for ax. Is it not axes? It's axes. Anyway, this is where I worked a few days. But there was a terrible, terrible supervisor, a German, who had-- he had to kill everybody. Every day, he had to kill somebody. He, you know, he was walking around with these long handles and just hitting people for no reason until they-- they got-- they were dead. And I said this-- I don't want to wind up dead that-- that way. So one day, I decided not to-- not to appear, not to go to work, not to appear in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you go to work in a concentration camp? Everybody's out of the barracks, assemble in a-- in a-- in a square in the center. They call it the Appellplatz. And from there, details went to different-- to different places in the-- in-- in the camp to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Instead of going to the Appellplatz, I was hiding under the barracks, because I didn't want to go back to that place because I knew one day I'm going to wind up dead from that-- from-- from a beating. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one day, two days, you know, I-- I managed to escape-- to escape that. But then on the third day-- it was May the 1st, because this was the transport that was going to-- they were assembling 500 men to go to Gross Rosen. We didn't know where to, but to be shipped out from Plaszow, and-- and I was on that list of 500. And they were looking for me, and I didn't appear. So they were looking. They were looking. They held up this-- this transport for three days, not-- probably not because of myself alone, because there were others who had the same idea. Eventually, they find-- they found me under the barracks. They pulled me out from there. They beat me up terribly, and they put me into this transport. And eventually, they marched us to the railroad station, and I was already on another transport to go-- know-- to go we didn't know where.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many days did you hide under the bunk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three days. I-- during the day, I-- I was-- during the night, I was out in the barracks, only during the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit more about the conditions of Plaszow. What was your barracks like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody had a bunk. There was no such-- in-- in other-- in other camps I-- I-- I was, three people shared one bunk. But over there, everybody had, and there was some straw. Some had some-- some blankets. They-- they were-- compared to others, they were-- the conditions were-- were more human.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you fed there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had rations. They-- we had bread every morning, and we had soup every evening. You couldn't survive a long time on these rations, but this was a camp where there was a lot of traffic, Polish people. It was a camp-- in a camp of Polish people. In-- in-- in our camp, there was a certain area surrounded with barbed wire where they only kept Polish people, some kind of a category of Polish people. I don't know what-- what they were. And-- and since another people were going out to work from the-- from the camp, so there was contact with other-- with Polish people, and they were able to bring in some food. So some-- some had it more better than others, those who were not just [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dependent on-- on-- on the rations.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in-- in-- in May, I was in a transport, and it turned out to Gross Rosen. In Gross Rosen, after they marched us from the train to the camp, we had to undress completely. They were examining us for any valuables.

speaker: interviewee
text: They examine-- they-- they looked into the rectum, into the mouth, whether you don't have anything there. And of course, they took everything if they found it. They beat people up if they found anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just go back a minute to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given a number in Plaszow at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Plaszow, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you-- were you there also given a uniform that you had to wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Plaszow? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You-- you had whatever you had. You-- you could wear that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people had uniforms, and some people didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't. But when you got-- how-- how long did it take you on this transport from Plaszow to Gross Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that ride like? Was that on a cattle car ride?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on a cattle car, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that ride? Same?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing, the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you couldn't escape from this.

speaker: interviewee
text: You couldn't escape. And then, I-- I-- I don't know where, I didn't have the stamina anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were beaten at this point, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- I didn't have--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: --the-- the courage. I didn't have the energy. It was different. I was already a number-- I-- I-- a person without a name. I-- I was degraded. I was dehumanized. I didn't have-- this-- it's-- it wasn't like 1940 that I had this-- the-- the new-- the courage and to do things and to-- to plan for myself. I know I lost everybody. I was alone. This-- what will be, will be.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of injuries did you sustain when they beat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible, bloody. I-- I didn't-- luckily, I didn't have any broken bones. Otherwise, I probably wouldn't have survived. But I was all blooded up, swollen, everything hurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why didn't-- it's interesting they didn't shoot you, that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --they just beat you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. It's interesting, yes. You never know. Because some people ask, how did you survive? You never know how you survived. I-- I mean, on so many occasions, by all rules of logic, I should have been dead. But I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this train ride took three days to get to-- to Gross Rosen.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Gross Rosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gross Rosen is where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is in-- in Silesia, in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had no idea where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- no. Even when I-- I arrived there, I didn't know what the name of the place is. Later, I found out it is Gross Rosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and you were immediately sent to shower and--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. First, you get undressed. You-- you put everything in there, and they examined you. And then you were standing outside for 18 hours at least. And then they put you into shower.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of the year is this now? Is this-- what--?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this was in May 1944, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were sent-- you were outside for 18 hours and then sent to shower.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And then you were distributed these striped clothing. This is the first time that I had striped clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you given a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: No number.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. And how about any hair shaved or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from your head?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yes. The hair-- the hair was shaved, yes. And-- and you were given these striped clothing and mostly wooden shoes. Some were lucky to- to find, you know, because they were throwing this out, and you-- you grabbed what you could grab. Same was with trousers and with a jacket [INAUDIBLE]. In that Gross Rosen, it-- we were only held there for three nights. But it was absolutely a terrible, terrible experience because every day they would come in and beat up people for no reason. You didn't know when you were next. And the rations were so-- so meager that you didn't know how long are you going to survive on this.
